metadata.summary: Automate deployment path from test suite to production. Structure as five jobs with stages, approvals, and strategies: rolling, blue/green, canary.

content.title: CD Pipelines and Deployment Strategies
content.summary: Structure as discrete jobs: build → integration → staging → E2E → production. Choose a deployment strategy: rolling, blue/green, or canary. Always emit DORA events with version, sha, and duration for observability.

why.p: Ad-hoc deploy.sh scripts lack observability, rollback, and approval gates. A structured pipeline makes every stage independently retry-able, captures DORA metrics automatically, and enforces human sign-off before production. Automatic rollback on smoke-test failure cuts mean time to restore from hours to minutes.

list.item: Authoring or refactoring .github/workflows/cd.yml (or GitLab CI / Buildkite equivalent) for a service.
Adding deployment-strategy primitives: blue/green, canary, rolling with proper readiness/liveness probes.
Wiring smoke tests, rollback triggers, deployment notifications, and DORA metric tracking.
Replacing ad-hoc deploy-gh.sh scripts with observable, gated pipelines.
Pure CI (build + test only) — this is CD-specific; see cd-basics for CI principles.
Full platform engineering (multi-cluster, cross-region, GitOps with Argo/Flux) — use pro/infra/cicd-engineer.
Serverless deploys managed by the vendor (Vercel, Netlify, Cloud Run) — those only need build config.
Monorepo selective deploys per package — requires Turborepo/Nx-aware pipelines not covered here.

section.name: CD Pipeline Structure — Five Stages and Safety Gates

rule.statement: Structure every CD pipeline as five independent jobs: build (unit tests + image), integration (service deps), staging deploy, E2E on staging, production deploy (manual approval).
rule.rationale: Each job is independently retry-able; failures are localized to a stage. Merged stages (e.g., build + staging deploy) make failure attribution ambiguous and rollback harder.

rule.statement: Set concurrency.group to deploy-${{ github.ref }} with cancel-in-progress: false so two concurrent pushes don't interleave deployments.
rule.rationale: cancel-in-progress: true would cancel an in-flight production deploy when a new push arrives, leaving the cluster in a partial state.

rule.statement: Tag Docker images with the commit SHA — never latest; rollback must be deterministic by SHA.
rule.rationale: latest is mutable; if the image is rebuilt between deploy and rollback commands, kubectl rollout undo deploys a different image than expected.

rule.statement: Use OIDC federation (GitHub Actions → AWS/GCP/Azure) instead of long-lived secrets stored in repository variables.
rule.rationale: Long-lived secrets have indefinite validity; OIDC tokens are short-lived, scoped to the specific job, and automatically rotated. Leaked OIDC tokens expire in minutes.

rule.statement: Pin every third-party action to its commit SHA, not a tag; treat supply-chain hygiene as a pipeline requirement.
rule.rationale: Tags can be force-pushed to point at malicious commits; SHA pinning guarantees the exact code that was reviewed is executed.

rule.statement: Gate the production job behind a GitHub environment with required reviewers; never wire the production deploy to run automatically without human sign-off.
rule.rationale: Autonomous production deploys remove the human checkpoint that catches last-minute context the pipeline cannot verify (security announcements, customer commitments, rollback readiness).

antipattern.description: Agent removes the manual approval from the production job to "make CI green".
antipattern.reason: Approval gates exist to protect production; removing them silently ships untested changes to end users. Block this pattern in code review.

rule.statement: Choose a deployment strategy based on rollback speed (rolling) vs traffic-split precision (canary) vs zero-overlap guarantees (blue/green).
rule.rationale: Rolling trades safety for speed — new pods start before old pods drain. Blue/green trades resources for safety — two full clusters running. Canary trades graduality for safety — slow traffic migration with automatic analysis.

rule.statement: Use rolling updates on Kubernetes: set maxUnavailable to 1, maxSurge to 2, and mandate readinessProbes and livenessProbes on the container.
rule.rationale: Without readiness probes, load balancers forward traffic to containers that are still booting. Without liveness probes, the cluster doesn't restart containers that hang.

example.code: spec: strategy: type: RollingUpdate rollingUpdate: maxUnavailable: 1 maxSurge: 2 template: spec: containers: - name: app readinessProbe: httpGet: path: /health/ready port: 8000 initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 10 livenessProbe: httpGet: path: /health/live port: 8000 initialDelaySeconds: 15 periodSeconds: 20

rule.statement: Blue/green via label swap: run two parallel deployments (blue + green), and switch traffic by updating the Service selector.
rule.rationale: Zero-overlap: old and new versions never coexist. Instant rollback: flip the selector back. Downside: 2x infrastructure cost during deployment.

rule.statement: Canary on Istio: shift traffic in stages (5% → 15% → 50% → 100%) and gate on SLO burn rate. Do not start with 50% on the first deploy.
rule.rationale: Canary trades gradual traffic migration for safety. SLO burn rate gating prevents cascading failures; HTTP 2xx alone is not sufficient.

rule.statement: Smoke tests must be the same script in staging and prod, parameterized only by base URL. Run them immediately after deployment; if they fail, automatically rollback.
rule.rationale: Smoke tests catch obvious breakage before end users see it. Automatic rollback cuts MTTR from "schedule a war room" to "the system recovered in 5 minutes."

example.code:
#!/usr/bin/env bash # scripts/deploy-with-rollback.sh set -euo pipefail NS="${1:?namespace}" DEP="${2:?deployment}" IMAGE="${3:?image:tag}" URL="${4:?smoke-url}"
PREV=$(kubectl -n "$NS" get deploy "$DEP" -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}') kubectl -n "$NS" set image "deploy/$DEP" "$DEP=$IMAGE" if ! kubectl -n "$NS" rollout status "deploy/$DEP" --timeout=5m; then kubectl -n "$NS" rollout undo "deploy/$DEP" exit 1 fi if ! curl -fsS --max-time 10 --retry 6 --retry-delay 5 "$URL/health/ready" >/dev/null; then echo "Smoke failed — rolling back to $PREV" kubectl -n "$NS" set image "deploy/$DEP" "$DEP=$PREV" kubectl -n "$NS" rollout status "deploy/$DEP" --timeout=5m exit 1 fi echo "Deploy ok: $IMAGE"

rule.statement: Emit DORA events (deploy_started, deploy_succeeded, deploy_failed) with version + SHA + duration to a single metrics sink before the pipeline changes tools.
rule.rationale: DORA metrics are only meaningful when instrumentation survives tool migrations. One event per deploy job, keyed by deployment ID, not per retry.

list.item: Use a planner agent that reads cd-basics first, then this file, then drafts the pipeline as discrete jobs (build, integ, staging, e2e, prod).
Each job becomes a small executor task: write YAML, validate with actionlint/gitlab-ci-lint, commit, watch the first run, fix failures.
Force a human approval gate before any production deploy job is wired up.
Capture the final pipeline run URL + DORA metric instrumentation as deliverables.
Never let an autonomous agent push to a production environment that lacks an approval rule.

antipattern.description: Agents tend to copy the kubectl set image snippet verbatim, missing image digest pinning and namespace scoping.
antipattern.reason: Require explicit --record, and use immutable image tags (commit SHA). Namespace isolation prevents cross-namespace pollution.

antipattern.description: Don't let an agent generate --force or --grace-period=0 flags.
antipattern.reason: They bypass safety nets and can orphan resources.

antipattern.description: Agents misuse continue-on-error: true to hide a real failure to make the pipeline "pass".
antipattern.reason: Review all uses of continue-on-error; most are masking real errors.

antipattern.description: Secrets: agents will paste tokens into workflow YAML during debugging.
antipattern.reason: Require a pre-commit secret scanner (gitleaks, trufflehop).

antipattern.description: Canary: agents will set weight: 50 on first deploy.
antipattern.reason: Force a 5%/15min initial step, with SLO burn-rate gating before promotion.

antipattern.description: Rollback: agents may rollback the K8s deployment but forget to roll back the schema.
antipattern.reason: Couple them; run migrations in a dedicated job before the deploy job.

antipattern.description: DORA: agents will compute deploy frequency without de-duplicating retries.
antipattern.reason: Instrument once at the gate, not per attempt.

antipattern.description: Rolling: agents drop readiness probes when the app starts slow.
antipattern.reason: Mandate initialDelaySeconds calibrated to real boot time.

antipattern.description: Approval gates: agents will helpfully "remove the manual approval to make CI green".
antipattern.reason: Block this in code review; approval gates protect production.

list.item: Add concurrency: { group: deploy-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: false } so two pushes don't deploy in parallel.
Use OIDC federation (GitHub → AWS/GCP/Azure) instead of long-lived secrets in repo.
Pin every action by SHA, not tag (actions/checkout@sha); supply-chain hygiene.
Image tag = commit SHA (immutable), not latest. Required for rollback determinism.
Smoke tests must be the same script in staging and prod, parameterized by base URL.
For canary, gate promotion on SLO burn rate (Honeycomb / Datadog / Prometheus alert), not just HTTP status.
Run schema migrations in a dedicated job before the deploy job, with its own rollback (or expand-contract pattern) — never inline in the app deploy.
Emit DORA events to a single sink so the metric pipeline survives tool changes.
Keep the prod job behind GitHub environment: production with required reviewers; agents cannot bypass.
GitHub Actions YAML in examples is illustrative: agents must add caching, concurrency control, OIDC for cloud auth, image-pull secrets.
Canary example uses Istio; if cluster runs Linkerd, NGINX-Ingress canary, or no service mesh, YAML must be rewritten.
Blue/green via label-swap is brittle for stateful services: db migrations + sticky sessions need extra orchestration.
Rolling-update example has single maxSurge: 2 / maxUnavailable: 1 — ignores PDBs, topology spread, zero-downtime requirements.
Smoke tests are referenced via ./scripts/smoke-tests.sh but script is undefined — agents must author and pin it.
Rollback strategy assumes kubectl rollout undo works; for canary/blue-green agents should swap traffic, not roll back deployment object.
Datadog DORA snippet is half-baked — change-failure-rate requires correlating to incidents, not just deploy outcome.
actionlint — static lint for GitHub Actions YAML (rhysd/actionlint)
gitlab-ci-lint (glab ci lint) — lint GitLab CI YAML (glab CLI)
gh workflow run / gh run watch — trigger + tail GHA runs from CLI (GitHub CLI)
kubectl rollout status / undo — wait/observe rollouts; manual rollback (kubernetes/kubectl)
kubeval / kubeconform — validate K8s manifests against schema (github.com/yannh/kubeconform)
helm template / helm lint / helm diff — render + diff charts before apply (helm.sh)
kustomize build — render overlays (kustomize.io)
argocd app sync / argocd app rollback — GitOps deploy/rollback (argo-cd CLI)
flagger — progressive delivery operator (canary, blue/green, A/B) (flagger.app)
kayenta / spinnaker — canary analysis (spinnaker.io)
dora-metrics (Google) / four-keys — compute DORA from event stream (github.com/dora-team/fourkeys)
cosign / syft / grype — sign images + SBOM + vuln scan in pipeline (sigstore.dev)
kubectl-tree / stern — inspect rollout tree + tail logs (krew)
GitHub Actions (SaaS) — best agent ergonomics: gh CLI + actionlint + reusable workflows
GitLab CI/CD (SaaS + OSS) — pipeline-as-code; environments + manual approvals built-in
Argo CD (OSS) — GitOps; agents commit manifests, controller deploys
Flux CD (OSS) — GitOps alternative; image-policy auto-bumps tags
Argo Rollouts (OSS) — canary + blue/green CRDs; pairs cleanly with Argo CD
Flagger (OSS) — automated canary + analysis on Linkerd/Istio/NGINX
Spinnaker (OSS) — heavy install; powerful canary
AWS CodeDeploy (SaaS) — built-in blue/green for ECS/EC2/Lambda
Google Cloud Deploy (SaaS) — Skaffold-driven progressive delivery
Octopus Deploy (SaaS) — strong UI, less agent-friendly than YAML-first tools
LaunchDarkly / Unleash / Flagsmith (SaaS / OSS) — decouples deploy from release
Datadog / Honeycomb / Grafana (SaaS / OSS) — pipeline emits deploy markers; SLO burn-rate gates rollout
PagerDuty / Opsgenie (SaaS) — wire deploy_failed to incident; track time-to-restore
Slack / Discord / Telegram (SaaS) — notify channels via webhook in final pipeline step

template.purpose: Bash script: set image → rollout-status → smoke → rollback on failure.
